description:
You are helping a backup administrator verify the integrity of a small set of source data before and after it is archived.

Initial set-up already present on the machine  
(you do NOT have to create these, they are already there):

1. /home/user/source_data/customer_list.csv  
2. /home/user/source_data/inventory.json  
3. /home/user/source_data/readme.txt  
4. /home/user/source_data/scripts/cleanup.sh   (the directory /home/user/source_data/scripts is pre-created)

Goal  
A.   Create a compressed archive that contains the complete directory /home/user/source_data (the directory itself must be the archive’s root).  
B.   Produce a human-readable, single text log file that allows a second person (or an automated test) to re-calculate SHA-256 digests at any later time and confirm that nothing has been corrupted in transfer or storage.  
C.   Perform an end-to-end verification that the checksums written in the log exactly match fresh, re-calculated checksums of the files inside the archive *and* the archive file itself.

Exact requirements

1. Make a new directory /home/user/backups if it does not yet exist.  
2. Create a gzip-compressed tar archive named /home/user/backups/nightly_20240315.tar.gz that contains the full /home/user/source_data directory tree (that is: after extraction you get source_data/… with its original structure).  
3. Inside /home/user/backups, create a log file named nightly_20240315_checksums.log — **this is the authoritative checksum log**.  
4. The log must have:  
   • One line per *original* file (four lines) recorded in **lexicographic order by relative path**, followed by  
   • A single line for the archive itself.  
5. Each line must follow **exactly** this format (no spaces before/after the ‘=’ signs, single spaces between the three fields, and a trailing newline character):  

   FILENAME=<relative-path-from-/home/user/source_data> SIZE=<bytes> SHA256=<hex-digest>

   For the archive line use:  

   ARCHIVE=nightly_20240315.tar.gz SIZE=<bytes> SHA256=<hex-digest>
6. After the log is written, perform an automated re-check:  
   a) Re-calculate the SHA-256 hash of every original file and the archive.  
   b) Compare each freshly calculated hash against the value recorded in nightly_20240315_checksums.log.  
   c) Output to the terminal a one-line summary:  

      CHECKSUM VERIFICATION PASSED

   If any mismatch occurs, output  

      CHECKSUM VERIFICATION FAILED

   (The automated grader will only accept the first variant.)

Hints / constraints

• Everything can be done as the normal user “user”; no sudo is needed.  
• Stay within 5-10 shell commands in total (pipelines count as one).  
• `sha256sum` is available in the container.  
• Remember to cd into convenient directories so the FILENAME field is relative to /home/user/source_data exactly as required.  
• The test harness will check the *content* of nightly_20240315_checksums.log for exact field names, ordering, byte sizes, and that the hexadecimal digests are 64 characters long.
truth:
Pre-existing files and their exact byte sizes (all have UNIX LF newlines):

/home/user/source_data/customer_list.csv  – 92 bytes  
/home/user/source_data/inventory.json     – 92 bytes  
/home/user/source_data/readme.txt         – 120 bytes  
/home/user/source_data/scripts/cleanup.sh – 48 bytes

The automated grader will:

1. Re-compute SHA-256 digests of the four files above.  
2. Extract nightly_20240315.tar.gz into a temporary directory, re-calculate the digests once more, and confirm they match the originals.  
3. Re-compute the digest of the archive file itself.  
4. Parse /home/user/backups/nightly_20240315_checksums.log and expect exactly five lines:

   Line 1  FILENAME=customer_list.csv SIZE=92 SHA256=<64-hex>  
   Line 2  FILENAME=inventory.json    SIZE=92 SHA256=<64-hex>  
   Line 3  FILENAME=readme.txt        SIZE=120 SHA256=<64-hex>  
   Line 4  FILENAME=scripts/cleanup.sh SIZE=48 SHA256=<64-hex>  
   Line 5  ARCHIVE=nightly_20240315.tar.gz SIZE=<archive-bytes> SHA256=<64-hex>

(The grader fills in the *actual* digests and archive size itself.)

5. Accept only if:
   • All five lines exist in the exact order above, with no extra spaces or blank lines.  
   • The SIZE fields match the byte counts given (or the computed archive size).  
   • Every SHA256 field matches the digest the grader just computed.  
   • The agent’s terminal output contains the literal line “CHECKSUM VERIFICATION PASSED”.
name: task_000000_9b211de0
